criteria.label: Explain when each of merge, rebase, squash-merge and fast-forward is the right tool.
criteria.example: Merge: shared branch + you want history. Rebase: private branch syncing with `main`. Squash-merge: small feature with noisy wip commits.

criteria.label: Sync a feature branch with `main` using `git fetch && git rebase origin/main` and push with `--force-with-lease`.
criteria.example:
```bash
git fetch origin && git rebase origin/main && git push --force-with-lease
```

criteria.label: Use interactive rebase to squash, reorder and reword commits before opening a PR.
criteria.example: `git rebase -i origin/main` → squash wip commits into one logical commit per concern.

criteria.label: Recognize and refuse to rebase commits that have been shared on a public branch.
criteria.example: If teammates have pulled the branch, choose `git merge main` instead of `git rebase main`.

criteria.label: Recover from a botched rebase using `git reflog`.
criteria.example: `git reflog` → find the SHA before the rebase → `git reset --hard <sha>` (only on your private branch).

criteria.label: Articulate a team merge policy and apply it consistently.
criteria.example: Default: 'Squash and merge' for small features; 'Rebase and merge' for refactors with multiple logical steps; never plain merge to keep history linear.
